senses.definition: The quality of being easy to reach, enter, or use.
examples: The accessibleness of the new library building has helped more students use it.
They improved the accessibleness of the website for users with disabilities.
Good signage increases the accessibleness of public transport.

senses.definition: The quality of being friendly and easy to talk to.
examples: Her accessibleness makes new employees feel comfortable.
The manager’s accessibleness encourages staff to share ideas.

senses.definition: Plural form of accessibleness; different kinds or examples of being easy to reach, use, or approach.
examples: The report compares the various accessiblenesses of public services in rural areas.
Designers must consider different accessiblenesses for users with diverse needs.

senses.definition: In a way that is easy to reach, enter, or use.
examples: The tools are stored accessibly on the wall near the workbench.
All emergency exits must be accessibly positioned.

senses.definition: In a way that is easy to understand or enjoy.
examples: The professor explained the theory very accessibly.
This complex topic is presented accessibly for general readers.

senses.definition: Reaching, entering, or using something, especially information or a system.
examples: You need a password for accessing the company network.
Many people have problems accessing affordable healthcare.
The app stopped accessing my location after the update.

senses.definition: The act of formally joining an organization, agreement, or treaty.
examples: The country’s accession to the trade agreement boosted exports.
Talks on accession to the union took several years.

senses.definition: The act of a person rising to a position of power, especially a throne.
examples: Her accession to the throne was celebrated across the country.
The film shows the king’s accession and early reign.

senses.definition: A new item added to a collection such as a library or museum.
examples: The painting is a recent accession to the museum’s collection.
Each accession is carefully recorded in the catalog.

senses.definition: Relating to accession, especially the act of joining or adding.
examples: The accessional documents were signed by all member states.
This chapter discusses the accessional process in detail.

senses.definition: Formally added an item to an official collection, such as a museum or library.
examples: The gallery accessioned three new sculptures last year.
All donated books are accessioned before they go on the shelves.

senses.definition: A person or organization that accedes to an agreement, treaty, or group.
examples: Each accessioner must accept the rules of the organization.
New accessioners were welcomed at the international conference.

senses.definition: The process of formally adding items to an official collection, such as a museum or library.
examples: Accessioning is a key part of managing museum collections.
The librarian is responsible for accessioning new books.

senses.definition: Plural of accession; new additions to a collection, organization, or agreement.
examples: The museum received several important accessions this year.
Recent accessions to the treaty changed its political balance.

senses.definition: Third-person singular form of accede; joins or agrees to something formally.
examples: The state accessions to the convention after lengthy debate.

senses.definition: A prize or special mention given to a student who almost wins first place.
examples: She received an accessit for her essay in the competition.
An accessit was awarded to the runner-up in each category.

senses.definition: Relating to or involving access or approach.
examples: The accessive features of the design were carefully tested.
Accessive rights were granted to all authorized users.

senses.definition: In a way that concerns or involves access or approach.
examples: The building is accessively planned for people with limited mobility.
These tools are accessively arranged for quick use.

senses.definition: Without access; not able to be reached, entered, or used.
examples: Many remote villages remain effectively accessless in winter.
An accessless website excludes users with disabilities.

senses.definition: A method or tool that allows access to data, especially in computing.
examples: The class provides an accessor for each private field.
Use this accessor to retrieve the current value safely.

senses.definition: Relating to something that is extra or additional, especially in transport or law.
examples: The invoice includes several accessorial charges.
Accessorial services such as packing are billed separately.

senses.definition: Extra things that are added to something to make it more useful or attractive.
examples: She bought matching accessories for her new dress.
The camera comes with several accessories, including two lenses.
We sell phone accessories such as cases and chargers.

senses.definition: People who help someone commit a crime but do not actually carry it out.
examples: Two accessories were arrested after the robbery.
The court found her guilty as one of the accessories.

senses.definition: Latin plural form used in some scientific or legal names meaning accessories or supplementary parts.
examples: The term accessorii appears in the full Latin title of the document.
Certain muscles are listed as accessorii in anatomical texts.

senses.definition: In a way that is secondary or additional to something main, especially in law.
examples: He was held accessorily liable for the damages.
The company was accessorily involved in the illegal scheme.

senses.definition: The quality of being additional or secondary to something main.
examples: The accessoriness of the detail keeps the main idea clear.
Legal scholars discussed the accessoriness of his role in the crime.

senses.definition: Plural of accessoriness; different ways or cases in which something is secondary or additional.
examples: These cases show different accessorinesses of minor actors in crimes.
The article examines various accessorinesses in complex contracts.

senses.definition: To add extra items, especially clothes or jewellery, to make something more attractive.
examples: She likes to accessorise her outfits with colourful scarves.
You can accessorise a simple dress with bold jewellery.
He accessorised the room with modern lamps.

senses.definition: Decorated or completed with extra items, especially in clothing or design.
examples: She wore an accessorised jacket with metal details.
The room looked beautifully accessorised with plants and cushions.

senses.definition: Third-person singular form of accessorise; adds extra items to make something more attractive.
examples: She accessorises every outfit with a statement necklace.
He accessorises his phone with a new case each season.

senses.definition: Adding extra items, especially clothes or jewellery, to make something more attractive.
examples: Accessorising can quickly change the feel of an outfit.
She spent the afternoon accessorising her new dress.

senses.definition: Latin form used in scientific names, typically meaning accessory or supplementary part.
examples: The muscle name includes the term accessorius.
In anatomy, accessorius often appears in Latin labels.

senses.definition: A rare or erroneous Latin-like form related to accessorii, usually seen only in technical or historical texts.
examples: The term accessoriusorii appeared in an old anatomical list.
Editors corrected accessoriusorii to the standard form in later editions.

senses.definition: To add extra items, especially clothes or jewellery, to make something more attractive.
examples: She loves to accessorize with bright earrings.
You can accessorize a simple outfit with a belt and scarf.
He accessorized his suit with a patterned tie.

senses.definition: Decorated or completed with extra items, especially in clothing or design.
examples: She wore an accessorized dress with a matching bag.
The living room was fully accessorized with cushions and artwork.

senses.definition: Third-person singular form of accessorize; adds extra items to make something more attractive.
examples: She accessorizes every dress with a unique necklace.
He accessorizes his outfits with hats and watches.

senses.definition: Adding extra items, especially clothes or jewellery, to make something more attractive.
examples: Accessorizing can turn a simple outfit into something special.
She spent time accessorizing before the party.

senses.definition: Plural of accessor; methods or tools used to access data, especially in computing.
examples: The library offers several accessors for the data structure.
These accessors let you read the values safely.

senses.definition: An extra thing that is added to something to make it more useful or attractive.
examples: A hat is a fun accessory in any wardrobe.
The phone comes with only one accessory, a charging cable.
We sell car accessory items such as seat covers.

senses.definition: Someone who helps another person commit a crime but does not directly do it.
examples: He was found guilty as an accessory to the robbery.
The police are still looking for an accessory in the case.

senses.definition: Providing extra help or support, especially in a secondary role.
examples: He was charged with accessory involvement in the crime.
The device includes several accessory features.

senses.definition: A very short musical note played quickly before or with a main note as a decoration.
examples: The pianist added an acciaccatura before the final chord.
An acciaccatura is usually written as a small note with a slash through its stem.
The piece uses many acciaccatura figures to create excitement.

senses.definition: Plural of acciaccatura; very short musical notes used as decorations before or with main notes.
examples: The score is full of acciaccaturas in the right hand.
She struggled at first to play the acciaccaturas cleanly.

senses.definition: Alternative plural of acciaccatura, meaning very short ornamental notes in music.
examples: The harpsichord part includes several acciaccature.
Students learned how to interpret acciaccature in Baroque pieces.

senses.definition: The part of grammar that deals with how word forms change, for example to show tense or number.
examples: The book begins with a section on accidence.
Students often find the accidence of Latin challenging.

senses.definition: Plural of accidence; different systems of word changes in grammar.
examples: The course compares the accidences of several languages.
Old and modern accidences are presented side by side.

senses.definition: Rare plural of accidency; accidental or changeable qualities of something.
examples: Philosophers distinguished between essences and accidencies.
These accidencies do not alter the core nature of the object.

senses.definition: A quality of something that is not part of its basic nature and can change without changing what it is.
examples: Color is often treated as an accidency rather than an essential property.
The argument focuses on the accidency of these features.

senses.definition: An unpleasant event that happens suddenly and is not planned, especially when people are hurt.
examples: He broke his leg in a car accident.
There was a serious accident on the highway.
Luckily, no one was hurt in the accident.

senses.definition: Something that happens by chance, not because it was planned or intended.
examples: Their meeting was pure accident.
The discovery of the medicine was an accident.

senses.definition: Happening by chance, not planned or intended.
examples: The damage was accidental, not deliberate.
They concluded that the fire was accidental.
It was an accidental meeting in the supermarket.

senses.definition: In music, a sign that shows a note must be played higher or lower than usual.
examples: The melody contains several accidentals.
Watch out for the accidentals in bar twelve.

senses.definition: The belief that events are mainly the result of accidents or chance rather than design.
examples: The novel reflects a kind of philosophical accidentalism.
Accidentalism challenges ideas of strict fate or destiny.

senses.definition: Plural of accidentalism; different versions or cases of believing that events are the result of chance.
examples: The article describes several historical accidentalisms.
Various accidentalisms appear in modern thought.

senses.definition: A person who believes that events happen mainly by accident or chance.
examples: As an accidentalist, she denies any deeper purpose in history.
The accidentalist argued that the result was nothing but luck.

senses.definition: Plural of accidentality; individual qualities or events that are accidental rather than essential.
examples: The writer focuses on the accidentalities of everyday life.
These accidentalities do not change the core argument.

senses.definition: The quality of being accidental and not part of the basic nature of something.
examples: He stressed the accidentality of these differences between people.
The accidentality of the event does not reduce its impact.

senses.definition: In a way that is not planned or intended.
examples: I accidentally deleted the file.
She accidentally called the wrong number.
He accidentally spilled coffee on his shirt.

senses.definition: The quality of happening by accident or not being planned.
examples: The accidentalness of their meeting changed both their lives.
He reflected on the accidentalness of the discovery.

senses.definition: Plural of accidentalness; different ways in which things can be accidental or unplanned.
examples: The story explores the many accidentalnesses of life events.
He listed the accidentalnesses that led to the final outcome.
